questionText: A curve has equation $y = x - \ln x$ for $x > 0$. Determine the values of $x$ for which the curve is increasing and the values for which it is decreasing.

steps.explanation: The term $\ln x$ is only defined for $x>0$, so we work on that domain; increasing/decreasing is decided by the sign of the gradient.
Using $\frac{d}{dx}(x)=1$ and the standard result $\frac{d}{dx}(\ln x)=\frac{1}{x}$.
Combining over a common denominator makes the sign of the gradient easy to read off.
On the domain $x>0$ the denominator is positive, so the sign of the gradient is the sign of the numerator $x-1$.
With a positive denominator, the fraction is positive exactly when $x-1>0$.
So the curve is increasing for $x>1$.
Similarly the gradient is negative when the numerator is negative.
Combining $x<1$ with the domain $x>0$ gives the interval $0<x<1$.
At $x=1$ the gradient is zero, separating the decreasing and increasing regions (a minimum).
So the curve falls on $0<x<1$ and rises for $x>1$, with a minimum turning point at $x=1$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Decreasing for $0<x<1$ and increasing for $x>1$ (minimum at $x=1$).
